attributes.stem:
A four-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of bloody diarrhoea. His parent reports he attended a petting zoo last week. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. His immunisations are up to date.

On examination, he appears lethargic. His pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.8°C. The abdomen is soft, not distended, with mild generalised tenderness. He is pale with mild peripheral oedema.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 82 g/L (115-145)

  • White cell count: 15.2 × 10⁹/L (4.5-13.5)

  • Platelet count: 45 × 10⁹/L (150-400)

  • Creatinine: 245 µmol/L (20-60)

  • Urea: 18.2 mmol/L (2.5-6.5)

  • Blood film shows numerous schistocytes
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This child presents with the classic triad of haemolytic uraemic syndrome (HUS): acute kidney injury, thrombocytopaenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (evidenced by schistocytes on blood film). The history of bloody diarrhoea following exposure at a petting zoo suggests Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli (STEC) as the cause. Haemolytic uraemic syndrome is the most likely diagnosis given these characteristic features.

Viral gastroenteritis typically causes watery rather than bloody diarrhoea and would not explain the triad of acute kidney injury, thrombocytopaenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia.

Immune thrombocytopaenia would cause isolated thrombocytopaenia but would not explain the acute kidney injury, haemolysis, or bloody diarrhoea. Schistocytes would not be present on blood film.

Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura shares many features with HUS including microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia but typically presents with predominant neurological symptoms (confusion, seizures, focal deficits) and less severe renal involvement. It is also rare in children and not associated with bloody diarrhoea.

Henoch-Schönlein purpura typically presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, and sometimes renal involvement. While it can cause gastrointestinal symptoms, it would not cause thrombocytopaenia or microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia.

The key to diagnosis is recognising the characteristic triad of HUS along with the preceding bloody diarrhoea and potential STEC exposure. Treatment is primarily supportive, including careful fluid management and renal support if needed. Antibiotics should be avoided as they may increase toxin release.
learningPoints: Shiga toxin-associated haemolytic uraemic syndrome typically presents with bloody diarrhoea followed by acute kidney injury, thrombocytopaenia, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia.
The combination of bloody diarrhoea, acute kidney injury, and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia is highly suggestive of HUS.
TTP is distinguished from HUS by predominant neurological symptoms and less severe renal involvement
references: Jokiranta TS. HUS and atypical HUS. Blood. 2017;129(21):2847-2856. doi:10.1182/blood-2016-11-709865
